section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: THE AGE OF A NEUTRAL ALLELE
nearby_text: highly asymmetric about the mean. Slatkin and Rannala (2000) provide an approximation for the cumulative probability for the age of an allele, given a frequency of p in a random sample of n alleles, $$ \Pr(t\leq\tau)=(1-p)^{-1+[n/(1+nN\tau)]} $$ (2.13)

label: Formula 2.14a
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: ALLELE-FREQUENCY DIVERGENCE AMONG POPULATIONS
nearby_text: first quantity on the right is $ p_0(1 - p_0) $. The quantity $ E[p_t(1 - p_t)] $ is half the expected heterozygosity in a population in generation t, which was already defined in Equation 2.5. Thus $$ \sigma_{p}^{2}(t)=p_{0}(1-p_{0})\left[1-\left(1-\frac{1}{2N}\right)^{t}\right] $$ (2.14a) which is well approximated by $$ \sigma_{p}^{2}(t)\simeq p_{0}(1-p_{0})(1-e^{-t/2N}) $$ for $N > 10$. This shows that the among-population variance asymptotically approaches $p_0(1 - p_0)$, which is

  "nearby_text": "first quantity on the right is $ p_0(1 - p_0) $. The quantity $ E[p_t(1 - p_t)] $ is half the expected heterozygosity in a population in generation t, which was already defined in Equation 2.5. Thus $$ \\sigma_{p}^{2}(t)=p_{0}(1-p_{0})\\left[1-\\left(1-\\frac{1}{2N}\\right)^{t}\\right] $$ (2.14a) which is well approximated by $$ \\sigma_{p}^{2}(t)\\simeq p_{0}(1-p_{0})(1-e^{-t/2N}) $$ for $N > 10$. This shows that the among-population variance asymptotically approaches $p_0(1 - p_0)$, which is"
}

label: Formula 2.15
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: HIGHER-ORDER ALLELE-FREQUENCY MOMENTS
nearby_text: In the case of neutrality, there are no directional forces, so $ E(\delta p) = 0 $, and the expected frequency of an allele remains perpetually at its initial value $ (p_{0}) $, $$ E(p_{t})=p_{0} $$ (2.15)

label: Formula 2.16a
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: HIGHER-ORDER ALLELE-FREQUENCY MOMENTS
nearby_text: and noting that $ E(p_{t})=p_{0} $, this expression can be rearranged to give the recursion equation $$ E[p_{t+1}^{2}|p_{t}]-p_{0}=[E(p_{t}^{2})-p_{0}]\lambda_{1} $$ the general solution of which is $$ E(p_{t}^{2})=p_{0}-[p_{0}(1-p_{0})]\lambda_{1}^{t} $$ (2.16a)

}

label: Formula 2.16d
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: HIGHER-ORDER ALLELE-FREQUENCY MOMENTS
nearby_text: which prove to be particularly useful (Crow and Kimura 1970): $$ E(p_{t}^{3})=p_{0}-\frac{3}{2}p_{0}(1-p_{0})\lambda_{1}^{t}-\frac{1}{2}p_{0}(1-p_{0})(2p_{0}-1)(\lambda_{1}\lambda_{2})^{t} $$ (2.16c) $$ \begin{aligned}E(p_{t}^{4})&=p_{0}-\frac{18N-11}{10N-6}p_{0}(1-p_{0})\lambda_{1}^{t}-p_{0}(1-p_{0})(2p_{0}-1)(\lambda_{1}\lambda_{2})^{t}\\&\quad+p_{0}(1-p_{0})\left(p_{0}(1-p_{0})-\frac{2N-1}{10-6}\right)(\lambda_{1}\lambda_{2}\lambda_{3})^{t}\end{aligned} $$ (2.16d) where $ \lambda_{i}=1-(i/2N) $. Modifications for these expressions for populations with separate sexes and 1:1 sex ratios are given by Lynch and Hill (1986).

label: Formula 2.19
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: LINKAGE DISEQUILIBRIUM
nearby_text: the frequency of recombination between loci, and $ \lambda_1 = 1 - (1/2N) $, the expected disequilibrium (under random mating) resulting from the joint forces of recombination and gametic sampling is $$ \begin{aligned}E(D_{t})&=[(1-c)\lambda_{1}]^{t}D_{0}\\&\simeq D_{0}e^{-(2Nc+1)t/(2N)}\end{aligned} $$ (2.19)

label: Formula 2.20
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: LINKAGE DISEQUILIBRIUM

nearby_text: sly, $ \lambda_{i}=1-[i/(2N)] $ (Hill and Robertson 1968). The evolutionary variance of D associated with drift among replicated populations or among loci starting from the same allele frequencies is $$ \sigma^{2}(D)=E(D^{2})-E^{2}(D) $$ (2.21)

label: Formula 2.22
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: LINKAGE DISEQUILIBRIUM
nearby_text: Hill and Robertson (1968) introduced a standardized measure of linkage disequilibrium, often referred to as the squared within-gamete correlation of allele frequencies at two loci $$ r^{2}=\frac{D^{2}}{p(1-p)q(1-q)} $$ (2.22)

label: Formula 2.23
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: MUTATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
nearby_text: qually mutationally exchangeable. On the other hand, homozygotes will be mutationally converted to heterozygotes at a rate of $ 2u $. Thus, the expected dynamics of heterozygosity can be expressed as $$ H_{t+1}=H_{t}\lambda_{1}\left((1-2u)+\frac{2u(k-2)}{k-1}\right)+2u(1-\lambda_{1}H_{t}) $$ (2.23)

label: Formula 2.24a
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: MUTATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
nearby_text: Setting $ H_{t+1} = H_t $, the expected value of heterozygosity under drift-mutation balance is found to be $$ E(H)=\frac{\theta}{1+[\theta k/(k-1)]} $$ (2.24a) where $ \theta = 4N u $, a result first given by Kimura (1968a). Note that $ \theta $ has the pleasing interpretation of being the ratio of the rates of mutational production of heterozygotes from ho

label: Formula 2.24b
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: MUTATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
nearby_text: of loss of heterozygosity by drift (1/2N). label: Formula 2.26a
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: MUTATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
nearby_text: l that Equation 2.25a defines a beta distribution (Appendix 2), as many of its properties are already well known. For example, the mean of the distribution, which is the expected allele frequency, is $$ E(p)=\frac{\beta}{\theta+\beta}=\frac{1}{k} $$ (2.26a) and the allele-frequency variance among replicates is $$ \sigma^{2}(p)=\frac{\theta\beta}{(\theta+\beta)^{2}(\theta+\beta+1)}=\frac{k-1}{k^{2}[2N u k(k-1)+1]} $$ (2.26b)

        "section": "Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction",
        "subsection": "MUTATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM",

  "nearby_text": ". For example, the mean of the distribution, which is the expected allele frequency, is $$ E(p)=\\frac{\\beta}{\\theta+\\beta}=\\frac{1}{k} $$ (2.26a) and the allele-frequency variance among replicates is $$ \\sigma^{2}(p)=\\frac{\\theta\\beta}{(\\theta+\\beta)^{2}(\\theta+\\beta+1)}=\\frac{k-1}{k^{2}[2N u k(k-1)+1]} $$ (2.26b)"
}

label: Formula 2.27a
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: MUTATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
nearby_text: Expressions for the variance of heterozygosity for a population en route to equilibrium were derived by Li and Nei (1975) and Lessard (1981), and at equilibrium $$ \sigma^{2}(H)=\frac{2\theta[1+(\theta/\ell)]}{[1+\theta+(\theta/\ell)]^{2}[2+\theta+(\theta/\ell)][3+\theta+(\theta/\ell)]} $$ (2.27a) where $ \ell = k - 1 $ (Stewart 1976). Note that as $ k \to \infty $ (the infinite-alleles model), $$ \sigma^{2}(H)=\frac{2\theta}{(1+\theta)^{2}(2+\theta)(3+\theta)} $$ (2.27b) which reduces to abou

subsection: MUTATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
nearby_text: nucleotide sites $ \theta $ is generally substantially smaller than one, whereas for sites separated by hundreds of base pairs or more, $ \rho $ is generally greater than one. label: Formula 2.31a
section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
nearby_text: Several useful results follow from Equation 2.30a. First, the probability of a monomorphic sample is $$ \Pr(k=1)=\frac{(n-1)!}{(\theta_{L}+1)(\theta_{L}+2)\cdots(\theta_{L}+n-1)} $$ (2.31a)

label: Formula 2.31b
section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
nearby_text: Second, a bit of algebra gives the mean and variance for the number of alleles in a sample as $$ \begin{align*}E(k)=1+\theta_L\cdot\sum\limits_{j=2}^n\frac{1}{\theta_L+j-1},\qquad\sigma^2(k)=\theta_L\cdot\sum\limits_{j=1}^{n-1}\frac{j}{(\theta_L+j)^2}\end{align*} $$ (2.31b)

  "nearby_text": "Second, a bit of algebra gives the mean and variance for the number of alleles in a sample as $$ \\begin{align*}E(k)=1+\\theta_L\\cdot\\sum\\limits_{j=2}^n\\frac{1}{\\theta_L+j-1},\\qquad\\sigma^2(k)=\\theta_L\\cdot\\sum\\limits_{j=1}^{n-1}\\frac{j}{(\\theta_L+j)^2}\\end{align*} $$ (2.31b)"
}

label: Formula 2.32
section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
nearby_text: , one allele appears as a singleton, whereas the other two alleles both appear as two copies, giving $ n_1 = 1 $, $ n_2 = 2 $, and $ n_3 $, $ n_4 $, $ n_5 = 0 $. The constraint on the $ n_i $ is that $$ \sum_{i=1}^{n}i\cdot n_{i}=n $$ (2.32)

label: Formula 2.33a
section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
nearby_text: y powerful result, due to Ewens (1972) and Karlin and McGregor (1972), is that the joint probability distribution of $ n_{1}, \cdots, n_{n} $ and k is given by the Ewens sampling formula (Ewens 1972) $$ \Pr(n_{1},n_{2},\cdots,n_{n},k\mid n)=\frac{n!\theta_{L}^{k}}{S_{n}(\theta_{L})\left(1^{n_{1}}2^{n_{2}}\cdots n^{n_{n}}\right)n_{1}!n_{2}!\cdots n_{n}!} $$ (2.33a)

  "nearby_text": "y powerful result, due to Ewens (1972) and Karlin and McGregor (1972), is that the joint probability distribution of $ n_{1}, \\cdots, n_{n} $ and k is given by the Ewens sampling formula (Ewens 1972) $$ \\Pr(n_{1},n_{2},\\cdots,n_{n},k\\mid n)=\\frac{n!\\theta_{L}^{k}}{S_{n}(\\theta_{L})\\left(1^{n_{1}}2^{n_{2}}\\cdots n^{n_{n}}\\right)n_{1}!n_{2}!\\cdots n_{n}!} $$ (2.33a)"
}

label: Formula 2.33b
section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
subsection: The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum
nearby_text: that the sample is monomorphic, Equation 2.31a, directly follows by setting $ k = 1, n_1 = n $. Equations 2.30a and 2.33a show that the conditional distribution of $ n_1, \cdots, n_n $ given $ k $ is $$ \Pr(n_{1},n_{2},\cdots,n_{n}\mid n,\ k)=\frac{n!}{S_{n}^{k}\left(1^{n_{1}}2^{n_{2}}\cdots n^{n_{n}}\right)n_{1}!n_{2}!\cdots n_{n}!} $$ (2.33b)

nearby_text: derived nucleotides in the population.         "id": "formula_2.16b",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}E[p_{t+1}^{k}|p_{t}]&=E[(p_{t}+\\delta p)^{k}|p_{t}]\\\\&=\\sum_{i=0}^{k}\\binom{k}{i}p_{t}^{i}E\\left[(\\delta p)^{k-i}|p_{t}\\right]\\end{aligned}",
        "label": "Formula 2.16b",
        "section": "Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction",

        "latex": "E(p_{t}^{3})=p_{0}-\\frac{3}{2}p_{0}(1-p_{0})\\lambda_{1}^{t}-\\frac{1}{2}p_{0}(1-p_{0})(2p_{0}-1)(\\lambda_{1}\\lambda_{2})^{t}",
        "label": "Formula 2.16c",
        "section": "Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction",

        "label": "Formula 2.16d",

        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\Pr(k\\mid\\theta_L,n)=\\frac{S_n^k\\;\\theta_L^k}{S_n(\\theta_L)}\\end{align*}",

        "section": "THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION",

      {

        "latex": "S_{n}(\\theta_{L})=\\theta_{L}(\\theta_{L}+1)(\\theta_{L}+2)\\cdots(\\theta_{L}+n-1)",

        "section": "THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION",

        "id": "formula_2.33a",
        "latex": "\\Pr(n_{1},n_{2},\\cdots,n_{n},k\\mid n)=\\frac{n!\\theta_{L}^{k}}{S_{n}(\\theta_{L})\\left(1^{n_{1}}2^{n_{2}}\\cdots n^{n_{n}}\\right)n_{1}!n_{2}!\\cdots n_{n}!}",
        "label": "Formula 2.33a",
        "section": "THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION",
        "subsection": "The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum",
        "position": 58,
        "symbols_defined": [

        "latex": "\\Pr(n_{1},n_{2},\\cdots,n_{n}\\mid n,\\ k)=\\frac{n!}{S_{n}^{k}\\left(1^{n_{1}}2^{n_{2}}\\cdots n^{n_{n}}\\right)n_{1}!n_{2}!\\cdots n_{n}!}",
        "label": "Formula 2.33b",
        "section": "THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION",
        "subsection": "The Infinite-alleles Model and the Associated Allele-frequency Spectrum",
        "position": 59,
        "symbols_defined": [

        "id": "formula_2.34a",
        "latex": "\\phi(x)=\\frac{\\theta}{x}\\quad for\\quad\\frac{1}{2N}\\leq x\\leq1-\\frac{1}{2N}",
        "label": "Formula 2.34a",
        "section": "THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION",
        "subsection": "The Infinite-Sites Model and the Associated Site-frequency Spectrum",
        "position": 60,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\phi",
          "x"
        ]
      }
    ]
  },
  "nearby_text": "derived nucleotides in the population. Reconfiguring Equation 2.34a for unpolarized alleles, so that $ 0 < x \\leq 0.5 $ where x is the minor-allele frequency, gives the folded Watterson distribution, $$ \\phi(x)=\\frac{\\theta}{x}+\\frac{\\theta}{1-x}=\\frac{\\theta}{x(1-x)}\\quad for\\quad\\frac{1}{2N}\\leq x\\leq1/2 $$ (2.34b)"
}

label: Formula 2.35a
section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
subsection: The Infinite-Sites Model and the Associated Site-frequency Spectrum
nearby_text: ows from the Watterson distribution. In the case of an unfolded frequency spectrum, for a sample of size n, the number $ s_{i} $ of the L total sites with i derived nucleotides has the expected value $$ E(s_{i})=\frac{\theta_{L}}{i},\quad for\quad1\leq i\leq n-1 $$ (2.35a)

nearby_text: dix 4) require the full distribution within a sample, not just the expected value. The probability of seeing exactly k derived nucleotides at a site with allele-frequency x follows from the binomial, $$ \Pr(k\mid x,n)=\binom{n}{k}x^{k}(1-x)^{n-k} $$ (2.36a)

label: Formula 2.36b
section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
subsection: The Infinite-Sites Model and the Associated Site-frequency Spectrum
nearby_text: hat a sample is polymorphic at a site with allele-frequency x is just one minus the probability of a sample monomorphic for either the derived $ (x^{n}) $ or the ancestral $ (1 - x)^{n} $ nucleotide, $$ \Pr(1\leq k\leq n-1)=1-x^{n}-(1-x)^{n} $$ (2.36b)

section: THE DETAILED STRUCTURE OF NEUTRAL VARIATION
subsection: The Infinite-Sites Model and the Associated Site-frequency Spectrum
nearby_text: The probability of seeing k derived nucleotides at a random site in a sample is the average of Equation 2.36a over the possible x values, $$ \Pr(k\mid n)=\binom{n}{k}\int_{1/(2N)}^{1-1/(2N)}x^{k}(1-x)^{n-k}\phi(x)dx $$ (2.36c) where $ \phi(x) $ is defined by Equation 2.34a. label: Formula 2.38
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: THE GENEALOGICAL STRUCTURE OF A POPULATION
nearby_text: The mean coalescence time for two randomly sampled genes is simply $$ \bar{t}_{c}(2)=\sum_{t=1}^{\infty}t\cdot P_{c}(t)=2N $$ (2.38)

label: Formula 2.39
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: THE GENEALOGICAL STRUCTURE OF A POPULATION
nearby_text: cence for any pair in the sample in the preceding generation is simply the product $p_n/(2N)$. Thus, the probability distribution for the coalescence time of one pair within a set of $n$ sequences is $$ P_{c}(p_{n},t)=[1-(p_{n}/2N)]^{t-1}[p_{n}/(2N)] $$ (2.39)

  "nearby_text": "$ (p_n=1) $, which again has an expected coalescence time of 2N generations (Figure 2.10). The implication of these results is that the expected time for merging n random lineages into n-1 lineages, $$ \\bar{t}_{n}=2N/p_{n}=\\frac{4N}{n(n-1)} $$ (2.40) increases with decreasing sample size."
}

label: Formula 2.41
section: Neutral Evolution in One- and Two-Locus Systems: Introduction
subsection: THE GENEALOGICAL STRUCTURE OF A POPULATION
nearby_text: The total expected genealogical depth of a sample, obtained by summing the expectations of each coalescence event, is $$ \begin{align*}\bar t_c(n)=\sum_{i=2}^n{4N\over i(i-1)}=4N\left(1-{1\over n}\right)\end{align*} $$ (2.41)

  "nearby_text": "The total expected genealogical depth of a sample, obtained by summing the expectations of each coalescence event, is $$ \\begin{align*}\\bar t_c(n)=\\sum_{i=2}^n{4N\\over i(i-1)}=4N\\left(1-{1\\over n}\\right)\\end{align*} $$ (2.41)"
}

label: Formula 2.42
section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
subsection: Quantifying Population Structure: $ F_{ST} $
nearby_text: total metapopulation is just $ p_0(1 - p_0) $. $ F_{ST} $ is then defined as the fraction of the total variance attributable to the variance in the frequency of allele B among demes, $ \sigma^2(p) $, $$ F_{ST}=\frac{\sigma^{2}(p)}{p_{0}(1-p_{0})} $$ (2.42)

  "nearby_text": "total metapopulation is just $ p_0(1 - p_0) $. $ F_{ST} $ is then defined as the fraction of the total variance attributable to the variance in the frequency of allele B among demes, $ \\sigma^2(p) $, $$ F_{ST}=\\frac{\\sigma^{2}(p)}{p_{0}(1-p_{0})} $$ (2.42)"
}

label: Formula 2.43
section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
subsection: Quantifying Population Structure: $ F_{ST} $
nearby_text: e expected variance in the allele frequency between two populations separated from a common ancestor t generations in the past (ignoring mutation and assuming no migration among groups), we have that $$ F_{ST}=\left[1-\left(1-\frac{1}{2N_{e}}\right)^{t}\right]\simeq\frac{t}{2N_{e}}\quad for\quad t\ll N_{e} $$ (2.43)

  "nearby_text": "e expected variance in the allele frequency between two populations separated from a common ancestor t generations in the past (ignoring mutation and assuming no migration among groups), we have that $$ F_{ST}=\\left[1-\\left(1-\\frac{1}{2N_{e}}\\right)^{t}\\right]\\simeq\\frac{t}{2N_{e}}\\quad for\\quad t\\ll N_{e} $$ (2.43)"
}

label: Formula 2.44
section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
subsection: Quantifying Population Structure: $ F_{ST} $
nearby_text: at $ F_{ST} $ values are closely related to the average coalescence times of alleles within subpopulations, $ \bar{t}_{0} $, and of alleles drawn randomly from the entire metapopulation, $ \bar{t} $, $$ F_{S T}=\frac{\overline{t}-\overline{t}_{0}}{\overline{t}}=1-\frac{\overline{t}_{0}}{\overline{t}} $$ (2.44)

label: Formula 2.45a
section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
subsection: Mutation-migration-drift Equilibrium Values of $ F_{ST} $
nearby_text: 1971; Nagylaki 1982). Provided there is some potential migratory route between all demes, regardless of the level of migration, the mean coalescence time between random pairs of genes within demes is $$ \bar{t}_{0}=2N d $$ (2.45a) generations, i.e., twice the sum of the demic population sizes (Li 1976; Strobeck 1987; Hey 1991; Nagylaki 2000).   "nearby_text": "1971; Nagylaki 1982). Provided there is some potential migratory route between all demes, regardless of the level of migration, the mean coalescence time between random pairs of genes within demes is $$ \\bar{t}_{0}=2N d $$ (2.45a) generations, i.e., twice the sum of the demic population sizes (Li 1976; Strobeck 1987; Hey 1991; Nagylaki 2000). Under this model, the independence of the within-deme variation of the migration rate"
}

section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
subsection: Mutation-migration-drift Equilibrium Values of $ F_{ST} $
nearby_text: les to coalesce into the same deme. Unlike the amount of variation within demes, the differentiation among demes clearly depends on m. Substituting Equations 2.45a and 2.45b into Equation 2.44 yields $$ F_{ST}=\frac{1}{1+4N\frac{md^{2}}{(1-d)^{2}}}\simeq\frac{1}{1+4Nm}\quad for\quad d\gg1 $$ (2.46)

label: Formula 2.47b
section: MUTATION-MIGRATION-DRIFT EQUILIBRIUM
subsection: Mutation-migration-drift Equilibrium Values of $ F_{ST} $
nearby_text: et al. 2009b). Substituting in the values for the various mean coalescence times noted previously, and assuming $d$ and $g$ moderately large and $m_{2} \ll m_{1}$, results in further simplification, $$ \begin{aligned}&F_{DG}\simeq\frac{1}{1+4Nm_{1}}\\&F_{GT}\simeq F_{ST}\simeq\frac{1}{1+4Ndm_{2}}\\ \end{aligned} $$ (2.47b)
